title: Eight-Day Belonging Montage + Sol 58 Corridor Catch
summary: Eight Sols pass before Peter notices. Catch-side-pass-1 comes together. Sol 53 launch trajectory phase clears tolerance band; Vasquez folds his timing tweak into cradle parameters with no note. Sol 55 Oren begins saving him a galley seat by which empty chair is nearest the bench. Sol 56 Peter stops looking for the soft yellow warning flag in the corner of his left display — it has not been there since Sol 50. He cannot remember when the work became his. Some evening Kessler stops at his workstation, looks at the catch-side trajectory cluster, and says 'That cluster is doing everything I wanted it to do.' Walking back to his cabin Peter realizes he is happy. Sol 58 1830 Kessler catches him in the corridor: 'Galley. You're behind on a dinner I owe you. Come.'
text_excerpt: Eight days passed before he noticed they had passed. ... 'Galley. You're behind on a dinner I owe you. Come.'

title: Galley Dinner with Six of Nine
summary: The galley is warmer than during Peter's first week. Six of nine present: Vasquez at one end, Oren and a lab assistant under the recyclers, an older materials-team woman, the supply pilot's relief, and Kessler at the head with a glass of amber. The food is unimproved; the company has reorganized the room. Oren teases Peter mid-meal — 'Tell me you do not name your variables after stars' / 'You named the catch-side branch after a hand of cards.' / 'Pass-1 is what a poker player says before he raises in pass-2' / 'Pass-1 is what an engineer says before he opens pass-2 and finds out the first one wasn't right.' The lab assistant snorts into her drink. Vasquez, flat: 'He's been arguing with the integrator about it for eight days. I've stopped reading his commit messages.' Peter sees, for the first time, the corner of her mouth do something that in another woman would be a smile. He takes a second helping of the unimpressive stew and finds he is hungry.
text_excerpt: The galley was warmer than it had been ... and found, to his small interior surprise, that he was hungry.

title: Walk to Kessler's Office; the Photograph and the Grandfather Question
summary: Kessler tips his head toward the corridor: 'Walk me back. I want to show you something.' In the office, door closed and working terminal dark, he takes down the reef photograph Peter has seen before (Sol 44, shallow water lit from beneath) and sets it flat on the bare wood beside the coral fragment. The corridor light through the open transom is enough; he does not turn on the desk lamp. 'Your grandfather. On your resume. You said his name was Kamau Mwangi.' 'Yes.' 'Coastal Kenya. Mombasa or thereabouts.' 'Mombasa.' 'He would have been alive when this reef was alive.' Peter does not answer; Kessler does not seem to require him to.
text_excerpt: When the table had thinned, Kessler set his glass down ... Kessler did not seem to require him to.

title: Kessler Personal Reveal — Earth Six Times, Miriam, Andaman, Defunding, Wife, 'Build Something Instead'
summary: Kessler tells the long answer in his easy table-register. Earth six times, 2510-2522, five years cumulative; technically one of the last three living men to have done significant fieldwork on the planet — the other two are dead. Marine biologist before he was anything else; his grandmother Miriam was a marine biologist who told him about coral reefs and bioluminescence and the sea at night, in a village three meters underwater by the time he was twelve. She died when he was twelve. The fragment on the desk is hers — kept on her writing table for fifty-one years; his mother kept it for him; he has kept it on a desk wherever he has had a desk. The last expedition documented the dying of the Andaman Fragment — bleach line three meters in seven days; a notebook of raw field notes, no metaphor, never re-read since 2523; a paper journals declined as 'not bearable.' Mars Government reclassified Earth as resource extraction zone in 2525; his department was defunded the next quarter; he lost his Olympus chair. His wife left in 2527. He rented a basement lab in Tharsis Industrial 2525-2535 — borrowed equipment, foundation money, and the part of the work that mattered, which was the part foundation money would not fund. In 2535 a private donor estate (the controlling interest in this base) made him an offer that did not require him to defend a budget. He smiles — small, dry, entirely without bitterness, which is the most disturbing thing about it. The short answer: 'I was a paleontologist of a sort, which is to say a man who specialized in counting the things humanity broke before they were finished evolving, and at some point, after enough years of counting, you either lie down in the count or you decide to do something the count cannot reach.' He picks up the coral fragment off its stand — the first time Peter has seen him touch it. 'I spent my life watching humanity destroy the most beautiful thing in the universe. This project is my chance to build something instead.' He sets the fragment back. The room is quiet enough to hear the working terminal's cooling fan asleep.
text_excerpt: 'I went to Earth six times,' Kessler said ... The room was quiet enough to hear the working terminal's cooling fan asleep.

title: Mirror Beat and Parting
summary: Peter looks at the photograph; he does not look at Kessler. He understands, in the small undeclared way one understands these things, that Kessler told him this not because the project required it but because Kessler had been waiting for someone in the building who could be told it without being lost. He understands, in the same undeclared way, that the man across the desk is a possible future of the man in this chair — twenty years on, the same brilliance, the same isolation, the same set of hands on a desk in a borrowed room — and that the difference between them, the only difference, is that one of them had decided to do something and the other had spent six months in a Mars apartment closing job applications. He does not say any of that. He says, 'Thank you for telling me.' 'Thank you for being someone I could tell.' Kessler stands. 'Sleep, Peter. Tomorrow's the integration window with Vasquez's cradle. I'll need you sharp.' 'I'll be sharp.' 'And Peter — ' Kessler pauses at the office door, hand on the frame, expedition photographs ranked behind him in the corridor light. He does not look back. 'We'll talk about the third thing soon.'
text_excerpt: Peter looked at the photograph on the desk ... 'We'll talk about the third thing soon.'

title: Return to Nav Lab + Routine Check + Epoch Cluster Anomaly + Comparison Tool Loaded
location: Nav Lab, Kuiper Belt Asteroid Base
summary: Peter walks back through sleeping corridors. The residential level hums with night-air recyclers. The gray-flour bay is closed. He passes the door without slowing. At his workstation he stands with his hand on the chair-back and lets the day land. Then he sits, opens catch-side-pass-1, and does what he has done for eight nights — a quiet pre-bed look at the launch trajectory cluster, a routine stellar position consistency check. The check returns the residuals he expected; the cluster holds under tolerance. He sits with the result for a long second, the way he sat in Kessler's office twenty minutes before. Then, on a habit of attention he could not have explained at the time, he asks the integrator a different question — not whether the cluster holds, but how tightly the reference epochs themselves are distributed across the stellar position catalogue. The cover story called for a generic envelope, several million years deep, deliberately uncorrelated with the present. The reference epochs are not distributed across that envelope. They are clustered. Tightly. Around a single point in deep time, with a residual scatter narrower than the cover story's design tolerance permitted. Not a smear. A line. He looks at it for a count of seconds he later cannot remember the length of. Then, without opening another tab, without saying anything to himself in words, he reaches up and pulls down the stellar-cartography comparison tool from the menu — the tool he has not used since Helix, never run on this base because there had been no reason to — and queries the cluster's barycentric center against the historical epoch catalogues he loaded onto his datapad eight years ago and never deleted. The catalogues are dense. The query is specific. A small progress bar opens in the corner of his left display, where the warning flag used to live, and begins, quietly, to load.
text_excerpt: Peter walked back to the Nav Lab through corridors that were sleeping ... and began, quietly, to load.
